text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE / MOLECULAR IMAGING
68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT -- WHOLE BODY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Gerald Hutchinson is a 71-year-old male with known prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5), originally staged cT3bN1M0 (AJCC Stage IVA), treated with androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide acetate) plus abiraterone/prednisone and definitive pelvic IMRT. He now presents with biochemical castration-resistant prostate cancer: serial PSA values rising from a nadir of 0.4 ng/mL to 2.3 ng/mL over approximately 6 months, with a PSA doubling time of roughly 3.5 months. Testosterone confirmed castrate (9 ng/dL). Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant identified on myRisk panel. Conventional imaging (Tc-99m bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained contemporaneously) was negative for definitive metastatic disease. PSMA PET/CT requested to evaluate for occult metastatic disease and to guide systemic therapy selection.

PROCEDURE:
Following intravenous administration of 6.2 mCi of 68Ga-PSMA-11, whole-body PET/CT imaging was performed from skull vertex to mid-thigh. Diagnostic-quality, contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained for anatomic co-registration and standalone CT interpretation. Images were reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with maximum intensity projection (MIP) reconstruction. No adverse reactions to radiopharmaceutical administration.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
Physiologic PSMA uptake noted in the bilateral parotid and submandibular salivary glands, lacrimal glands, and nasal mucosa -- all within expected normal biodistribution. No abnormal PSMA-avid cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No intracranial lesions identified on CT. Calvarium unremarkable.

CHEST:
Physiologic PSMA uptake in the liver and spleen within normal limits. The lungs are clear on CT without evidence of pulmonary nodules, consolidation, or pleural effusion. No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No PSMA-avid foci within the thorax. Post-radiation changes are not present in this region. No axillary adenopathy.

ABDOMEN:
Liver demonstrates uniform physiologic PSMA uptake. No focal hepatic lesions identified on CT or by discrete PSMA-avid foci. Gallbladder, pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys unremarkable. Bilateral kidneys show the expected strong PSMA expression in the proximal tubules, consistent with normal physiologic biodistribution. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the level of the aortic bifurcation. No ascites.

PELVIS -- LYMPH NODES:
There is a focus of moderately increased PSMA uptake in the right iliac chain corresponding to a short-axis 1.1 cm lymph node on CT (SUVmax 4.2). This node was previously identified on staging MRI as suspicious for nodal involvement; the current PSMA avidity is above background and localizes precisely to this node. The intensity of uptake, while not markedly elevated, is clearly above physiologic vascular and soft tissue background. Two additional smaller right iliac chain nodes (previously noted at 0.9 cm and 0.7 cm on staging MRI) are no longer distinctly enlarged on current CT; no PSMA-avid foci are seen corresponding to these nodes, suggesting interval response in the smaller nodes in the setting of prior ADT and radiation.

PROSTATE BED / SEMINAL VESICLE REGION:
Post-treatment changes in the prostate bed. There is faint, heterogeneous residual PSMA activity in the prostate bed region, which is difficult to distinguish with certainty from physiologic urinary bladder activity and post-treatment fibrosis. No discrete mass-like PSMA-avid focus is identified in the prostate bed above background. Urinary bladder is adequately filled. No periurethral or perivesical soft tissue abnormality.

BONE:
There is a single focus of mildly increased PSMA uptake projecting over the right posterior iliac crest (SUVmax 3.8). On CT, this corresponds to a subtle area of subtle sclerosis/early cortical irregularity measuring approximately 8 mm, not convincingly identified as a definitive lesion on CT alone, and not seen on the contemporaneous Tc-99m bone scan. The level of PSMA avidity at this site, while not markedly elevated, is above surrounding marrow background and is morphologically discrete. This is suspicious for an early osseous metastatic deposit. No other abnormal bone uptake is identified. The previously documented right obturator / right iliac chain lymph nodes are addressed above. The axial and appendicular skeleton is otherwise unremarkable without lytic or sclerotic lesions on CT. No pathological fracture.

VISCERAL ORGANS:
No PSMA-avid hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral lesions. No peritoneal deposits. No ascites.

INCIDENTAL CT FINDINGS:
Stable mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine. No acute abdominal pathology. No aortic aneurysm. Small bilateral inguinal lymph nodes within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:

1. PSMA-avid right iliac chain lymph node (1.1 cm, SUVmax 4.2), consistent with residual/recurrent nodal metastatic disease. This node had prior morphologic features suspicious for involvement on staging MRI and retains PSMA avidity despite prior ADT and radiation therapy.

2. Single focus of mildly increased PSMA uptake at the right posterior iliac crest (SUVmax 3.8) without convincing CT correlate and not seen on contemporaneous bone scan. This finding is highly suspicious for an early bone metastasis, particularly in this clinical context of rising PSA and CRPC.

3. No visceral metastases. No brain or skull base lesions.

4. No evidence of neuroendocrine dedifferentiation pattern (lack of diffuse somatostatin receptor-like or neuroendocrine distribution; PSMA expression maintained).

Overall, these findings are consistent with low-volume metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) detectable by PSMA PET/CT, with disease involving one regional lymph node and one suspected early osseous site. This PSMA PET/CT upstages the patient relative to conventional imaging, which was negative for definitive metastatic disease.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Signature on file]
Nuclear Medicine / Molecular Imaging
